breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: select new employeetimesheet of unmaterialized db.servicereports query; .OrderBy().ToList() boundary at L144 AFTER projection -> server-side. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy (archive L134) DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs at line 139, replace the untranslatable CLR helper with an EF-native nullable-DateTime AddDays that EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD, mirroring legacy EF6 DbFunctions.AddDays semantics:

  endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1) : a.endtime,

Rationale: `DateTime.AddDays` on the value of a nullable column is recognized by EF Core's SqlServer provider and translates to DATEADD(day, 1, endtime). `.Value` is safe here because the branch is only taken when `a.endtime < a.starttime` evaluates true, which requires `a.endtime` to be non-null. The result type stays `DateTime?` (implicitly widened) and is assigned to the `DateTime? endtime` member, preserving the original output exactly. This matches the legacy behavior where `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` returned the shifted nullable date. No other change needed; the where-clause `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay` is already correct.
reasoning:
I attempted to refute this flag but independently confirmed it is a genuine EF Core 10 port regression that 500s.

SERVER-SIDE confirmed (Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs, method `weekly`/employee-timesheet action, query at L124-153): The projection containing the flagged expression at L139 â€” `endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime` â€” sits inside `select new employeetimesheet { ... }` which is part of the chain `(from a in db.servicereports join b... join c... where(EF.Functions.DateDiffDay...) select new employeetimesheet {...}).OrderBy(o => o.EmployeeName).ToList()`. The FIRST materialization boundary is the `.ToList()` at L144 â€” it comes AFTER the projection. No `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` precedes the projection, and this is not a top-level standalone query nor an IGrouping aggregate. So the `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` call is composed into the IQueryable that EF Core 10 must translate to SQL. The later `.ToList().Select(...)` at L144+ (computing hours/minute) runs client-side and is irrelevant to the break.

WOULD 500 confirmed: `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain CLR static method (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`), NOT a registered EF function (no [DbFunction] attribute, no HasDbFunction mapping). Its argument `a.endtime` is a real mapped column (Models/ProTask.cs L35: servicereport.endtime is `DateTime?`), so EF Core cannot client-evaluate the call inside the server projection. EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary user-defined static method to SQL, so it throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" at execution â†’ HTTP 500. This fires on ANY non-empty result row (realistic data), hence high severity. The conditional ternary itself (`a.endtime < a.starttime` comparison and the `: a.endtime` branch) is translatable; it is solely the `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` call that breaks translation.

PORT REGRESSION confirmed: legacy EF6 (_archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs L134) used `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` â€” the canonical EF6 function that translates to SQL DATEADD. The port replaced it with the untranslatable CLR shim. The surrounding where-clause was correctly ported to EF Core native `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay` (vs legacy `DbFunctions.DiffDays`), confirming only the AddDays helper was left incompatible. App worked in EF6; broken in the port.

The hunter's flag is accurate on all points (server-side, port regression, 500 cause).

In Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs, getemployeeworkinghour, line 540, replace:

    endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime,

with EF-native DateTime arithmetic that EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD:

reasoning:
CONFIRMED â€” the hunter's flag is correct. I independently verified both required conditions.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs, method getemployeeworkinghour(long empid, DateTime date) (L527). The query at L530-545 is `var sr = (from a in db.servicereports join b in db.servicereportmembers join c in db.Employees where ... select new employeetimesheet { ... endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime, ... }).OrderBy(...).ToList()...`. The first materialization boundary (`.ToList()`) is at L545, AFTER the projection. So the DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays call (L540) is composed into the IQueryable that EF Core 10 must translate to SQL. It is NOT after a materialization boundary, NOT a standalone top-level query, NOT a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. Server-side confirmed.

(b) WOULD 500: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR static method â€” Helpers/SendMailShim.cs L68-71: `public static class DbFunctionsCompat { public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0); }`. It is NOT a registered/[DbFunction]-mapped EF function. The overload bound here is AddDays(DateTime?, int?) since the entity `servicereport` (db.servicereports -> Models/ProTask.cs:28) has `endtime`/`starttime` as `DateTime?` (ProTask.cs L34-35). EF Core 10 cannot translate an arbitrary user static method embedded in a column projection that references server columns (a.endtime). It throws InvalidOperationException 'The LINQ expression ... could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. The conditional appears in EVERY projected row, so it fails whenever the query returns any rows (i.e. the employee has any servicereport in the day window) -> fires on realistic data, HIGH severity.

PORT REGRESSION: Confirmed by side-by-side with legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs. The legacy getemployeeworkinghour (L557) is byte-identical except L570 uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that translates to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped DbFunctions.AddDays -> DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays (untranslatable CLR helper). Pure port-break.

Note: there is a sibling identical occurrence in the same file at getemployeeworkinghourall (legacy L662, ported region around the second projection) that has the same defect and should be fixed too, but the flagged line 540 is itself a genuine confirmed 500.

reasoning:
I could not refute this finding; the code independently confirms it. File: Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs, line 632, inside method getemployeeabsent(long empid, DateTime date) (method spans L594-~773).

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The query `var sr = (from a in db.servicereports join b in db.servicereportmembers ... join c in db.Employees ... where ... select new employeetimesheet { ... endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime, ... })` is built over three real DbSets. The FIRST materialization boundary is `.OrderBy(o => o.EmployeeName).ToList()` at L637, which comes AFTER the projection (L626-636). Nothing materializes before it â€” no `.ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable()` precedes the projection, it is not a standalone top-level query, and it is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. So `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` at L632 is composed into the IQueryable and EF Core 10 must translate it to SQL.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” CONFIRMED. `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain static CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L68-71: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`). I verified it is NOT registered as an EF function: a repo-wide search for `HasDbFunction`/`DbFunctionAttribute`/method-call translator overrides returns nothing. An unmapped static CLR method inside a server-side projection is untranslatable in EF Core 10, which throws InvalidOperationException ("The LINQ expression ... could not be translated"). The ternary translates to a SQL CASE; EF attempts to translate both branches, and the AddDays branch is the untranslatable node. This 500s regardless of data (it fails at translation time, before SQL executes) whenever the surrounding `if (firstentrytask < firstentryapp)` block is entered.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs:662 is byte-for-byte the same query but uses canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)`, which EF6 maps to SQL DATEADD. The port swapped in the unmapped `DbFunctionsCompat.*` helper. entity servicereport.endtime/starttime are DateTime? (Models/ProTask.cs:34-35).

CAVEAT (real-world impact, not correctness): the enclosing method getemployeeabsent appears to be DEAD CODE â€” no caller exists in the live tree (callers at L224/225/402/494 invoke getemployeeworkinghour / getemployeeworkinghourall, never getemployeeabsent). So in practice this path may never execute today. But the flagged code itself is a genuine, server-side, EF-Core-10-untranslatable port regression that WILL 500 if the method is ever invoked, so I confirm rather than refute. (Note also: the same DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays pattern exists at the live-tree's getemployeeworkinghourall L843-equivalent path and other variants â€” those should be swept too, but are outside this single flagged line.)

reasoning:
I attempted to refute but independently confirmed the finding. File Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs, method getemployeeworkinghourall(long empid, DateTime date), line 813.

SERVER-SIDE â€” CONFIRMED. The query is `(from a in db.servicereports join b in db.servicereportmembers ... join c in db.Employees ... select new employeetimesheet { ..., endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1) : a.endtime, ... }).OrderBy(o => o.EmployeeName).ToList().Select(...)`. db.servicereports is `DbSet<servicereport>` (IQueryable, confirmed in Models/ApplicationDbContext.cs:67). The flagged ternary at L813 sits INSIDE the `select new employeetimesheet { }` projection, and the FIRST materialization boundary is `.OrderBy(o => o.EmployeeName).ToList()` at L818 â€” AFTER the projection. There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()` between the `select` and the enumeration. Moreover the projection is followed by a server-side `.OrderBy(o => o.EmployeeName)` over a projected member, which forces EF to translate the whole projection to SQL. It is not a standalone top-level local, not a post-materialization Select, and not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. So the fragment is genuinely server-side.

500 â€” CONFIRMED. DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR static helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) â€” NOT a registered EF function (the class comment itself says "EF Core has no EF.Functions.AddDays; client-evaluated"). EF Core 10 cannot translate this method call over the column expression `a.endtime`, and because the projection must be translated (server-side OrderBy over its member), it throws InvalidOperationException "The LINQ expression ... could not be translated" â†’ HTTP 500. This fires whenever getemployeeworkinghourall takes the `firstentrytask < firstentryapp` branch with a service-report present, i.e. realistic data â†’ HIGH severity.

PORT REGRESSION â€” CONFIRMED. Legacy _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs:843 (same method, identical query shape) used the canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)`, which EF6 translates to SQL DATEADD and runs server-side without error. The port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays. Entity types confirmed in Models/ProTask.cs (servicereport.starttime / .endtime are `DateTime?`), employeetimesheet in Models/Employee.cs.

Output-preservation of the fix: legacy DbFunctions.AddDays(DateTime?,int) returns DateTime? (null-propagating). The EF-native fix a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1) translates to DATEADD(day,1,[endtime]) and gives identical results, and the `.Value` cannot NRE here because the ternary guard `a.endtime < a.starttime` evaluates false (SQL 3-valued logic) when endtime is NULL, routing to the `: a.endtime` branch â€” so the AddDays branch is only taken when endtime is non-null. Behavior preserved.

why: select new of unmaterialized db.servicereports query; .ToList() boundary at L1019 after projection -> server-side. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs at line 1015, replace the untranslatable CLR shim with an EF-native DateTime method that translates to SQL DATEADD:

  endtime = (a.endtime < a.starttime) ? a.endtime.Value.AddDays(1) : a.endtime

Rationale / output-preservation: `endtime` is `DateTime?`. The conditional only takes the AddDays branch when `a.endtime < a.starttime`, which requires both to be non-null, so `a.endtime.Value` is safe in that branch. `(DateTime?).Value.AddDays(int)` is translated by EF Core 10 to DATEADD(day, 1, [endtime]), exactly mirroring the legacy EF6 `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` (which also produced DATEADD and preserved a nullable result â€” the false branch `: a.endtime` keeps the projected property type `DateTime?`). This is identical in result shape and value to the working EF6 query and removes the only untranslatable node in the projection (the WHERE's `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay` already translates fine).
reasoning:
I independently confirmed the hunter's claim at Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs:1015, method getalltime(long empid, string fromdate, string todate).

(a) SERVER-SIDE â€” confirmed. The query (lines 1000-1018) is built from `db.servicereports` joined to servicereportmembers/Employees with no intervening materialization. The `select new { ... }` projection containing the flagged expression (line 1015) closes at line 1019 where `.ToList()` is the FIRST materialization boundary. Therefore the projection â€” including `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)` â€” is part of the IQueryable and EF Core 10 must translate it to SQL. The trailing `.Select(...)` at lines 1019-1029 (the `(o.endtime - o.starttime).Value.Hours` arithmetic) runs client-side and is irrelevant.

(b) WOULD 500 â€” confirmed. `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` (Helpers/SendMailShim.cs:70) is a plain CLR static method `date?.AddDays(days ?? 0)`, NOT a registered EF function and NOT `EF.Functions.*`. EF Core 10 has no translation for an arbitrary user static-method call inside a projection, so the query fails at SQL-compile time with "could not be translated" â†’ HTTP 500. This is translation-time, not data-dependent: it throws even on zero rows, the instant `v.Count()` (line 1033) or `v.ToList()` (line 1034) triggers compilation. Severity HIGH (fires on every realistic call, not an edge case). Note the WHERE clause uses `EF.Functions.DateDiffDay` (lines 1004/1006) which DOES translate â€” it is specifically the projection helper that breaks the query.

PORT REGRESSION â€” confirmed. Legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/taskcalendarController.cs:134 used the same expression with the canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays(a.endtime, 1)`, which EF6 translated to SQL DATEADD. The legacy query was also server-side (projection at 128-138 followed by `.ToList()` at 139). The port mechanically swapped the translatable canonical `DbFunctions.AddDays` for the untranslatable CLR shim `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays`, introducing the regression. Entity confirmed: servicereports.starttime/endtime are `DateTime?` (Models/ProTask.cs:34-35).

This matches the established port-break class from prior batches and the hunter's analysis is accurate on all three axes.
